bibliographic_data.title: Quorums Quicken Queries: Efficient Asynchronous Secure Multiparty Computation
bibliographic_data.abstract: We describe an asynchronous algorithm to solve secure multiparty computation (MPC) over n players, when strictly less than a 1/8 fraction of the players are controlled by a static adversary. For any function f over a field that can be computed by a circuit with m gates, our algorithm requires each player to send a number of field elements and perform an amount of computation that is ˜O( m/n + √n). This significantly improves over traditional algorithms, which require each player to both send a number of messages and perform computation that is Ω(nm). Additionally, we define the threshold counting problem and present a distributed algorithm to solve it in the asynchronous communication model. Our algorithm is load balanced, with computation, communication and latency complexity of O(log n), and may be of independent interest to other applications with a load balancing goal in mind.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents an asynchronous algorithm for secure multiparty computation (MPC) that significantly reduces the number of messages and computations required compared to traditional algorithms, especially in large distributed systems.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors define a threshold counting problem and present a load-balanced distributed algorithm to solve it. The algorithm ensures each player sends ˜O( m/n + √n) field elements and performs ˜O( m/n + √n) computations, with O(log n) latency.
outcomes: Developed an asynchronous MPC algorithm that significantly reduces message and computation complexity for large distributed systems.
Defined the threshold counting problem and provided a solution in the asynchronous communication model.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper addresses secure multiparty computation (MPC) in large, highly distributed systems. It introduces an efficient asynchronous algorithm where each player sends ˜O( m/n + √n) field elements and performs ˜O( m/n + √n) computations, significantly improving over traditional algorithms that require Ω(nm). The authors also define the threshold counting problem and present a load-balanced distributed solution. This work is particularly relevant for applications requiring secure computation in large networks.
